title: Which countries include mumps vaccines in their national vaccination programs?
subtitle: This shows which countries provide and recommend mumps vaccines through routine services. People may still be able to receive the vaccine if it's not in the routine schedule – it might be optional or available commercially.
caption:
The 2024 world map displays the status of mumps vaccine inclusion in national routine vaccination programs, categorized by whether the vaccine is administered across the entire country, not routinely administered, or available only in specific regions. The visualization uses a color-coded system where teal represents countries with nationwide routine administration, salmon pink indicates nations where the vaccine is not routinely administered, and orange marks countries with regional implementation.

Teal coverage dominates the map, encompassing the United States, Canada, Brazil, Argentina, the United Kingdom, France, Germany, Italy, Spain, Russia, China, Japan, South Korea, and Australia. This color fills nearly all of North America, South America, Europe, and East Asia, showing widespread integration of the mumps vaccine into standard public health schedules in these areas. In contrast, the salmon pink color covers the majority of the African continent, including Nigeria, Ethiopia, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and South Africa, as well as India, Pakistan, Indonesia, and Papua New Guinea. These nations fall into the category where the mumps vaccine is not part of the routine national schedule. A small number of countries appear in orange, signifying regional rather than national rollout; visible examples include Mexico and Saudi Arabia. Greenland is shaded with diagonal lines to denote missing data.

The geographic distribution shows a clear divide where high-income regions and large economies in the Americas, Europe, and East Asia have adopted the vaccine nationally, while large portions of Africa and South Asia have not included it in their routine programs. The pattern places the highest concentration of non-routine administration across sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South and Southeast Asia.
